visits.text: Auckland is built across 53 volcanic cones - no other city on earth can claim that. Climb to the crater rim of Mount Eden (Maungawhau) at 196 m elevation: the panorama over the isthmus, both harbours and the skyline is worth every step. At the bottom of the perfectly preserved crater, ancient Māori cultivation terraces are still visible from the rim.

Head down to Viaduct Harbour, a former fishing basin redeveloped in 2000 for the America's Cup. Today it's 30 restaurants and bars lining the marina, with direct views of superyachts. The New Zealand Maritime Museum, set on the waterfront, traces the history from early Polynesian explorers through to the great sailing regattas - Auckland genuinely deserves its nickname 'City of Sails'.

Wander through the Ponsonby neighbourhood, ten minutes from the CBD: restored Victorian villas, art galleries, independent cafés and local boutiques. It's Auckland's liveliest quarter for understanding contemporary Kiwi culture.

activities.text: Head up the Sky Tower for the 360° view - the tower rises to 328 m and remains the tallest structure in the southern hemisphere. The brave can sign up for SkyJump (guided freefall from the top). For everyone else, the glass-walled restaurant on the 52nd floor is more than enough.

In the evening, explore the waterfront from Viaduct to Wynyard Quarter: oyster bars, seafood restaurants and lively terraces facing the marina. It's one of the city's best spots for dinner before an early start tomorrow.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

places.name: Christchurch

visits.text: Akaroa, 85 km from Christchurch, is the only town founded by French settlers in New Zealand. In August 1840, 63 French and Germans arrived to colonise Banks Peninsula in France's name - but the British frigate HMS Britomart had preceded them by a few days with the Treaty of Waitangi in hand. The settlers finally established themselves under the British flag. The French memory is everywhere: Rue Jolie, Rue Lavaud, bistros serving bouillabaisse, and even the Tricolour flying from certain windows. Banks Peninsula is the eroded remnant of a volcano extinct 9 million years ago - the harbour is a drowned crater.

Akaroa's Hector's dolphins: these dolphins are the world's smallest (up to 1.4 m long) and one of the most endangered species - the South Island subspecies numbers fewer than 15,000 individuals. Akaroa harbour is one of the world's best places to see them. Operators offer nature cruises with over 98% detection rate, and swimming trips with dolphins are also available.

Return to Christchurch: arrival late afternoon. If time permits, a pass through the Botanic Gardens (75 ha) and a visit to the Cardboard Cathedral (2013), designed by Shigeru Ban after the 2011 earthquake, show how the city has reinvented itself. Christchurch has the South Island's most convenient international airport for departures.

activities.text: Scenic drive on Banks Peninsula coastal roads: from Akaroa, several country roads lead onto the ridges with 360° views over the harbours below. The peninsula is scattered with nearly-deserted bays accessible on foot from the road.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

map_keywords: Akaroa Harbour Banks Peninsula Hector dolphins

visits.text: Otago Peninsula stretches 25 km east of Dunedin. Taiaroa Head, at its tip, hosts the world's only mainland royal albatross colony - these birds can have a 3-meter wingspan and nest meters from visitors. The colony is active October to March. The interpretation center explains the scientific monitoring of banded birds.

Yellow-eyed penguins (hoiho) come ashore at Sandfly Bay late afternoon. A wooden viewing hide allows you to watch without approaching. They're the world's rarest penguins - fewer than 4,000 individuals survive. The walk to the hide from Sandfly Bay car park takes 15 minutes.

Dunedin Railway Station (1906) is often cited as New Zealand's finest station - a building of green limestone and dark granite adorned with mosaics. Dunedin's architecture generally bears the mark of Scottish settlers who founded the city in 1848: Victorian pubs, church spires, and even a castle (Larnach, 1871, accessible by bus or car from the peninsula).

activities.text: Operators offer guided evening outings to observe little blue penguins (kororā) on peninsula beaches. These tiny penguins (33 cm) are the world's smallest and return to shore after sunset. Unguided visits are prohibited to protect nests.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

places.name: Franz Josef

visits.text: Franz Josef Glacier (Kā Roimata o Hine Hukatere) descends from the Southern Alps to below 300 m elevation - one of the few glaciers worldwide to plunge to this level into temperate rainforest. The valley trail (5.4 km return) follows the glacial river bed to the terminal face with its blue ice towers. The final section is on unmarked terrain since glacier modifications - stop at safety barriers. Helicopter flights with glacier landing are the most impressive way to explore it.

Lake Matheson, 5 km from Fox Glacier village, is a kettle lake formed 14,000 years ago by Fox Glacier's retreat. The water is stained dark brown by tannins from the surrounding forest - this perfect mirror reflects Aoraki/Mount Cook (3,724 m, New Zealand's highest peak) and Mount Tasman (3,497 m) in optimal conditions. The complete circuit (1h30) passes Reflection Island, the best viewpoint. Sunrise and sunset are prime times.

activities.text: Helicopter flight with glacier landing: several operators offer 20 to 45-minute flights over both glaciers with névé landing - the aerial view over the Southern Alps and both ice caps is one of New Zealand's most spectacular.

Soak in Franz Josef hot pools: geothermal pools heated to 36-38°C surrounded by outdoor forest on the village outskirts allow well-deserved relaxation after the day's walking. Private pool option available.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

map_keywords: Franz Josef Glacier terminal face walk

visits.text: Christ Church Raukokore is an isolated Anglican church built in 1894 on a small rocky headland battered by the waves of Papatea Bay. It's so small and exposed it seems to rise from the sea. The New Zealand Automobile Association ranked it among the region's 101 must-see sites - penguins sometimes nest under the floorboards. It's one of the most iconic images of the East Cape road.

At Te Araroa, on the roadside, stands New Zealand's oldest pōhutukawa - 600 years old, with tentacular branches covering over 20 metres in diameter. The pōhutukawa is New Zealand's Christmas tree: it blazes with crimson red flowers in December. From Te Araroa, a 22 km track leads to East Cape lighthouse, relocated here in 1922 from the small offshore island (the original island was too unstable). You must climb 700 steps from the car park to reach the lighthouse - the Pacific view from the top is worth every step.

Gisborne is the first city in the world to greet the sun each morning. The Tairawhiti Museum, by the river, displays Māori and colonial collections including the helm from the Star of Canada, wrecked here in 1912. The Hawke's Bay vineyards begin on the city outskirts - Gisborne produces notably reputed chardonnay.

activities.text: Swimming and surfing on Gisborne's beaches: Wainui Beach, 5 km north of town, is one of the North Island's most renowned surf beaches. Locals gather there in late afternoon. The Pacific view from the dunes at sunset is particularly beautiful.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety

visits.text: Cathedral Cove - Mautohe - owes its English name to James Cook who, sailing through Mercury Bay in November 1769, thought the natural arch resembled a cathedral roof. Access is on foot (45 min from Grange Road, fully sealed marked track) or by water taxi from Hahei Beach. The giant arch connects two separate coves, and Te Hoho Rock, the stone pinnacle in the middle of the bay, is visible from inside. The 9 km² marine reserve surrounding the site is the country's sixth. Warning: there is a real rockfall risk under the arch - don't linger beneath it.

Hot Water Beach is a geological curiosity: buried ancient lava flow heats underground water that rises through the sand at temperatures reaching 64°C. The active zone is accessible only two hours either side of low tide. You dig your own natural spa with a spade (hire available at nearby cafés), then cool off in the ocean waves. The beach receives over 150,000 visitors per year - arrive early.

activities.text: Sea kayaking from Hahei Beach to Cathedral Cove: this is the most magical way to approach the cove, gliding under the arch at low tide. Local operators offer 3-hour guided trips including snorkelling stops in the marine reserve.

In the evening, stroll along Hahei Beach itself, a long golden sand beach with views to the Mercury Islands. The Ngāti Hei people inhabited these shores from 1350 AD and numerous pā (forts) are still visible on the headlands.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

visits.text: Kaikoura - 'eat crayfish' in Māori - sits at the intersection of the Kaikōura Range and an undersea canyon plunging over 1,000 m just kilometres offshore. It's this convergence of cold and warm currents, combined with upwelling from the depths, that attracts a concentration of marine life unique worldwide. The Point Kean fur seal colony is accessible on foot from the car park - some individuals regularly come to inspect parked cars.

The Peninsula Walkway circles the Kaikōura peninsula for 11 km (3h, free). The lookouts overlook the undersea canyon where sperm whales regularly surface. It's one of the South Island's most beautiful coastal walks, with views of the Seaward Kaikōura Ranges (the mountains plunge directly into the sea).

activities.text: Whale watching cruise with Whale Watch Kaikoura: giant sperm whales are permanent residents at Kaikōura year-round, feeding on giant squid in the canyon depths. Whale Watch Kaikoura, the only boat company of its kind in New Zealand, posts a 95% success rate and refunds 80% of the fare if no whales are sighted. You may also encounter dusky dolphins, orcas and albatrosses depending on season. Book several days ahead in high season.

Swimming with dusky dolphins: morning trips allow plunging with pods of dusky dolphins (groups of 100 to 1,000 individuals). These dolphins are particularly playful and acrobatic - Kaikōura is one of the world's best places for this experience.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

visits.text: Cape Reinga - Te Rerenga Wairua in Māori, 'the leaping place of spirits' - is the most sacred site in all of New Zealand. According to Māori tradition, the souls of the departed dive from the 800-year-old pōhutukawa clinging to the rocks to return to Hawaiki, the ancestral homeland. A one-kilometre path from the car park leads to the lighthouse and the famous headland where the Tasman Sea and Pacific Ocean collide in a visible swirling current. This isn't the northernmost point - that honour belongs to Surville Cliffs, inaccessible to the right on the horizon.

Ninety Mile Beach is actually only 88 km (55 miles) long - its name comes from European horsemen who estimated their horses covered 30 miles per day, forgetting that sand slows the pace. The beach is officially a national highway: 4x4 vehicles can drive on it, and tour buses regularly venture out between tides. Stop at Spirits Bay, a sheltered beach east of the cape, quieter and often deserted.

activities.text: Sandboarding on the Te Paki dunes: north of Ninety Mile Beach, immense sand dunes rise like desert mountains - a completely unexpected landscape in New Zealand. Local operators offer board hire. Allow about 20 minutes to climb for memorable descents.

The road to Cape Reinga follows the Aupōuri Peninsula, a narrow tongue of land with the Tasman Sea to the west and the Pacific to the east. Make photo stops at Rarawa Beach, reputed to have the whitest sand in all of New Zealand, and at Tapotupotu Bay beach, sheltered and perfect for a swim.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety

visits.text: The Ōpārara Basin, 20 km north of Karamea in Kahurangi National Park, is one of New Zealand's least-known curiosities. The Ōpārara Arch, the southern hemisphere's largest natural limestone arch, measures 219 m long, 79 m wide and 43 m high - the river carved it through 35-million-year-old limestone over more than a million years. Inside, you walk on fine sand banks while the tea-coloured river (stained by humic acids from the forest) flows silently below. The trail from the car park takes 25 minutes.

Moria Gate Arch, 20 minutes' walk from the same car park, is smaller (19 m high, 43 m wide) but its ceiling is completely carpeted with stalactites and hanging roots - the name comes straight from Lord of the Rings (as do several neighbouring streams: Galadriel Creek, Nimrodel). The adjacent Honeycomb Hill caves, accessible only on guided tours, have yielded some of the world's best-preserved moa bones - some dating back 20,000 years.

activities.text: Whitebait (galaxiidae) tasting: Karamea is one of New Zealand's last regions where whitebait fishing is still abundant (season September to October). These tiny transparent fish are a national delicacy - whitebait fritters (light egg patties) are available at local cafés like the Karamea Village Hotel.

Hike in the Ōpārara valley: beyond the arches, a 4.5 km one-way trail follows the river through primeval rimu and kahikatea forest - the same trees that populated forests in the dinosaur era. Blue ducks (whio), one of the country's rarest species, breed in the rapids.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety

places.name: Lake Tekapo

visits.text: Church of the Good Shepherd, built in 1935 from local stone to honour Mackenzie Basin pioneers, is one of New Zealand's most reproduced photographs. Its small window behind the altar frames the turquoise lake and mountains directly - by day. But at night this church becomes iconic: it's photographed with the Milky Way backdrop from the neighbouring sheepdog memorial. It's an active church - respect services in progress.

Aoraki Mackenzie International Dark Sky Reserve covers 4,367 km² including Aoraki/Mount Cook National Park and the Mackenzie Basin. Designated by the International Dark-Sky Association in 2012, it's the world's largest dark sky reserve and the southern hemisphere's only Gold-tier reserve. Level 2 on the Bortle scale means the Milky Way casts shadows in clear weather.

activities.text: Guided tour of Mount John Observatory: authorised operators bus visitors (private access closes after 5pm to preserve darkness) to the summit of Mount John (1,029 m) where the University of Canterbury operates New Zealand's only professional research observatory. Guides show southern constellations, visible planets and explain Māori astronomy (tātai aroraki). In cloudy conditions, a VR replacement tour is offered.

Soak in Tekapo Springs hot pools and stargaze lying in the pools: the 'star pool' formula combines guided astronomical observation with outdoor hot bathing at 37°C. It's one of New Zealand's most memorable night experiences.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

map_keywords: Church of the Good Shepherd Lake Tekapo

places.name: Marahau

visits.text: The ferry crossing from Wellington takes 3 hours and skirts the Marlborough Sounds - a network of sea inlets carved into forested hills. The view from the deck approaching Picton is among New Zealand's finest. At Picton, the South Island opens before you.

The drive from Picton to Marahau (around 2.5 hours) passes through Nelson, sunny capital of the South Island's warmest corner. Nelson averages over 2,600 hours of sunshine annually. The craft center and Saturday market are worth a stop if you're passing mid-day.

Abel Tasman National Park is New Zealand's smallest (22,530 hectares). It was created in 1942 at the initiative of conservationist Perrine Moncrieff, on the 300th anniversary of Dutch navigator Abel Tasman's arrival - who lost four crew members in a clash with Māori the day after anchoring. The park's coastline alternates golden sand beaches, granite cliffs, and subtropical forest.

Split Apple Rock is a granite boulder weighing several tons, split in two with millimeter precision by frost-wedging - ice made the rock explode from inside. It's visible from kayak or shoreline. It's one of the park's most photographed sites.
Cleopatra's Pool is a natural basin in a riverbed, with a water-polished rock slide. It sits 15 minutes' walk from the coast on a forest trail - a fine alternative in hot weather.

activities.text: Settle into Marahau late in the day: it's the gateway village to Abel Tasman National Park, on the coast. Arrange tomorrow's kayaking - most operators depart between 8am and 9:30am from the beachfront. Independent kayak rentals are also available for experienced paddlers.

A day of sea kayaking along the Coastal Track is Abel Tasman's signature experience. Paddling from Marahau to Anchorage Bay (4 hours) or Tonga Island Marine Reserve, you cross translucent waters, pass beaches inaccessible otherwise, and may spot seals, dolphins, and kaka parrots. Operators offer packages combining morning kayaking with water taxi return in the afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

map_keywords: Tonga Island Marine Reserve Abel Tasman

places.name: Mount Cook

visits.text: Lake Pukaki, on the road to Mount Cook, is one of the world's most spectacular glacial lakes. Its intense turquoise hue is caused by 'glacial flour' - microscopic rock particles suspended in meltwater from Tasman Glacier. The road follows the east shore for 55 km to Mount Cook village, with Aoraki (3,724 m, New Zealand's highest peak) growing with every kilometre.

Hooker Valley Track is the most popular hike in Aoraki/Mount Cook National Park. This 10 km return trail (3-4h, easy) crosses three swing bridges over the Hooker River and terminates at Hooker Lake, where icebergs float before the Hooker Glacier terminal face. It's the closest accessible point to the Mount Cook summit. The Tasman Glacier base, the country's longest (29 km), is visible to the side. In spring, the Mount Cook buttercup - the world's largest - blooms along the trail edges.

activities.text: Glacier Explorers on Lake Tasman: motorised zodiacs navigate between icebergs in the glacial lake at the foot of Tasman Glacier. The guide explains how the glacier's terminal face has retreated several kilometres since the early 20th century - the lake didn't exist yet in 1973.

Stargazing from Mount Cook village: at 900 m elevation, far from any light pollution, the Mackenzie sky is rated level 2 on the Bortle scale (one of the world's darkest skies). The Milky Way is visible to the naked eye most clear nights.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

map_keywords: Hooker Valley Track Aoraki Mount Cook
Tasman Glacier Aoraki Mount Cook National Park

places.name: Napier

visits.text: On 3 February 1931 at 10:47 am, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake levelled central Napier in two and a half minutes, killing 256 people. The fires that followed finished destroying the commercial districts. The reconstruction, conducted during the depths of the Great Depression in less than two years, naturally adopted the Art Deco style then in vogue, especially since its reinforced concrete buildings better withstood earthquakes. Napier now has the world's greatest concentration of Art Deco buildings - 144 buildings constructed between 1931 and 1937 out of 160 total remain intact. The National Tobacco Company Building (1933), with its arched entrance, dome and stained glass adorned with raupo, is the most photographed structure.

Marine Parade runs along the seafront for 2 km, lined with palms and Norfolk Island pines planted on land raised by the earthquake - the tremor literally created 4,000 hectares of new land by lifting the bay floor 2.7 m. The statue of Pania of the Reef, a local legendary figure, has stood at the water's edge since 1954 - she is to Napier what the Little Mermaid is to Copenhagen.

activities.text: Guided Art Deco walking tour with the Art Deco Trust: guides dressed in 1930s costume take you through shopping streets and interior lanes to decode the facades, chevron patterns and mosaics. The tour lasts about 1h30. If you pass through in February, the Tremains Art Deco Festival transforms Napier into a film set with vintage cars and residents in period costume.

Excursion to Hawke's Bay vineyards: 15 minutes from Napier, the region is New Zealand's second-largest wine area by surface. It specialises in chardonnay, pinot gris and red blends. Several estates offer on-site tastings in the afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety

map_keywords: Napier Art Deco precinct Emerson Street
Pania of the Reef statue Napier

visits.text: Huka Falls are on the Waikato River, 3 km north of Taupo: in 10 seconds, 220,000 litres of water funnel through a 15-metre canyon before dropping 11 metres. The water is an almost artificial turquoise blue. The viewing platform is a 5-minute walk from the car park - an easy stop en route to Tongariro.

Tawhai Falls - nicknamed 'Gollum's Pool' since filming Lord of the Rings in 2001 - are 3 km south of Whakapapa Village on SH48: a 13-metre waterfall drops into a circular basin surrounded by tree ferns. This is where Gollum's fishing scene was filmed. The track from the car park takes 20 minutes return - perfect for late afternoon before heading back to the village.

activities.text: The Tongariro Alpine Crossing is considered New Zealand's finest one-day hike: 19.4 km linear traverse across Mount Ngauruhoe volcano (2,291 m), with the Emerald Lakes - three craters filled with water coloured by hydrothermal minerals - as the highlight. Start from Mangatepopo (end of the shuttle bus) early morning, finish at Ketetahi late afternoon. The shuttle is mandatory: cars cannot be left at the start. Allow 6 to 8 hours depending on your pace, bring waterproof clothing and hiking boots - the terrain is volcanic with loose scree between 1,500 and 1,967 metres.

days_plan.itin_title: New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

places.name: Nelson

visits.text: Nelson is New Zealand's sunniest city - Blenheim sometimes competes, but Nelson usually wins over the year. The city centre with its art galleries, Saturday morning market (one of the country's liveliest) and independent cafés is particularly pleasant to explore. Christ Church Cathedral dominates the central hill.

Climb to the Centre of New Zealand Monument, the official geodetic point from which all topographic maps of the country were drawn. The climb from Queens Gardens takes 20 minutes on foot through bush. The view over Tasman Bay and surrounding ranges is remarkable.

Tahunanui Beach is Nelson's main beach, with 3 km of golden sand on Tasman Bay. Its waters are among the warmest on the South Island in summer. The sunset over the forested hills of the hinterland reflects in the shallow water of the bay.

activities.text: Visit artisan workshops in Nelson: the city is known for its strong artisan community - potters, glassblowers, jewellers and painters have established their studios here. Nelson Arts Gallery and Suter Art Gallery showcase the best of local creation. Bridge Street concentrates many of these studios.
Craft beer tastings: the Nelson region is New Zealand's hop capital - much of the hop used throughout the country is grown here. Several craft breweries offer tastings from the city outskirts.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety

map_keywords: Centre of New Zealand Monument Nelson

visits.text: Mount Taranaki rises to 2,518 m. This near-perfectly symmetrical stratovolcano is considered New Zealand's most beautiful and stood in for Mount Fuji in the film 'The Last Samurai' (2003). From central New Plymouth, on clear days, the snow-capped summit is visible from the coast. The Plateau Road allows you to drive up to the permanent snow zone at around 1,000 m.

The Len Lye Centre, opened in 2015, is the world's only museum entirely devoted to a kinetic artist - New Zealander Len Lye (1901-1980), pioneer of abstract cinema and animated sculpture. The undulating steel exterior is itself a sculpture. Inside, 'Wind Wand', his best-known work, stands on New Plymouth's seafront facing the ocean.
The Taranaki coast offers several fine viewpoints of the volcano from the sea. Te Rewa Rewa Bridge, a white arched footbridge over the estuary, is a postcard classic with Taranaki as backdrop.

activities.text: The walk around Mount Taranaki (Around the Mountain Circuit) covers 55 km and takes 3 to 5 days. But short trails from the Plateau or Dawson Falls allow you to see kāmahi and rātā forests, volcanic ravines, and alpine vegetation zones in under half a day. Local operators organize guided outings.

New Plymouth's coastal walkway runs 13 km along rocky coastline between Port Taranaki and Bell Block. The 3 km between downtown and Te Rewa Rewa Bridge are accessible on foot or by bike in 30 minutes and provide a complete reading of the relationship between city, sea, and volcano.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

visits.text: Ōamaru's Victorian Precinct is one of New Zealand's most intact preserved architectural ensembles. The white local limestone (Ōamaru Stone) façades mostly date from the 1870s-1890s, when the town enriched itself exporting wool and frozen meat. Push through the doors of old warehouses turned galleries, secondhand bookshops, and artisan workshops.

Steampunk HQ is a permanent art installation housed in a former freight station. The steampunk universe - Victorian retro-futurism based on gears and steam - has found its global temple in Ōamaru. Interactive sculptures, a modified locomotive, and temporary exhibitions make this unlikely venue one of the country's most singular museums.

Ōamaru's little blue penguin colony is New Zealand's most accessible. In the evening (generally from 7-8pm depending on season), hundreds of kororā march from the sea to their burrows in the limestone cliffs - the dusk procession, in dense groups, is a unique spectacle. Official grandstands allow observation without artificial light.

activities.text: Sunday's market in the Victorian Precinct gathers antique dealers, local makers, and artisans. Ōamaru is renowned for its cheeses - the local dairy offers tastings and workshop tours.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

visits.text: Pūrākaunui Falls tumble in three tiers over 20 meters amid podocarp forest. They feature on a New Zealand postage stamp and in nearly every guidebook to the country - not without reason. The return trail from the car park takes 20 minutes. Bring your camera.

Nugget Point is the Catlins' most iconic headland. The lighthouse, built 1869-1870, perches 76 m above the sea. Below, a series of gold-nugget-shaped rocks (the Nuggets) emerge from the waves. At Roaring Bay, just before the lighthouse, a viewing hide allows you to watch yellow-eyed penguins (hoiho) return from the sea late afternoon.

Curio Bay is famous for its 180-million-year-old petrified forest, one of only three fossil forests accessible worldwide. At low tide, silicified trunks and roots are visible on the lava rock. At the bay's other end (Porpoise Bay), Hector's dolphins regularly come to play in the surf - they're the world's smallest marine dolphins.

activities.text: The drive from Queenstown or Te Anau to the Catlins is long (3-4 hours) - it's a day of driving and successive stops. Plan the order of visits according to tides for Curio Bay and penguin arrival time at Nugget Point (dusk).

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

visits.text: Waitangi Treaty Grounds, 2 km from Paihia, is where it all began: on 6 February 1840, 46 Māori chiefs and representatives of the British Crown signed the Treaty of Waitangi here, the founding document of the New Zealand nation. The Treaty House, delivered as a kit from Sydney in 1834, was the residence of British Resident James Busby. Inside Te Whare Rūnanga, the carved whare built for the 1940 centenary, the ornaments represent tribes from across New Zealand. The ceremonial waka Ngātokimatawhaorua measures 35 metres and is launched every Waitangi Day.

Take the ferry from Paihia wharf to Russell (15 minutes), New Zealand's unofficial first capital. This is where whalers, traders and missionaries disembarked from the 1830s onwards. Christ Church (1836) is the country's oldest church - its walls still bear bullet marks from the 1845 wars. Pompallier Mission House, built in 1842, was the first Catholic establishment in the entire Asia-Pacific region.

activities.text: Join the Māori cultural evening at Waitangi Treaty Grounds: pōwhiri (welcome ceremony), haka, poi and a traditional hāngī meal - vegetables and meat slow-cooked in an earth oven. Available October to April, booking essential.

Bay of Islands sunset cruise: the 144 islands scattered across the bay are accessible by catamaran from Paihia. It's one of New Zealand's most prized sailing spots, and the late afternoon light on the forested islands is particularly beautiful.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety

visits.text: The Interislander ferry (3h Wellington to Picton) is a spectacle in itself: the Cook Strait crossing opens onto the Marlborough Sounds, a labyrinth of 1,500 km of submerged coastline created by rising waters 14,000 years ago at the end of the last ice age. Mountains plunge directly into the water from heights of several hundred metres, and successive sea arms reveal themselves at each turn. Settle on the outer deck.

From Picton, head out on the Queen Charlotte Drive, a 35 km scenic road winding between forested ridges and the coves of Queen Charlotte Sound to Havelock. Several lookouts open onto views that photos don't do justice. Havelock is nicknamed 'the green-lipped mussel capital of the world' - a seafood meal is mandatory here.

activities.text: Cruise in Queen Charlotte Sound from Picton: water taxis and excursion boats offer half-day trips into the most remote arms of the Sound, with dolphin, seal and seabird watching. James Cook anchored at Ship Cove - accessible only by sea - five times between 1770 and 1777; his memorial is still visible there.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

visits.text: Punakaiki's Pancake Rocks are New Zealand's most photographed geological formation. These 30-million-year-old limestone pillars - built layer by layer from marine shell debris - were uplifted by tectonics and carved into distinct slices by alternating hard and soft layers. At high tide with a southwesterly swell, the sea surges into underground galleries and shoots through vertical vents in columns of water and spray. The boardwalk circuit is 1.1 km - allow 45 minutes.

Truman Track, 3 km north of the Pancake Rocks, is a 30-minute trail through nīkau palm forest (New Zealand's only native palms) emerging onto a spectacular pebble beach with cliffs, caves and marine arches. Less crowded than Pancake Rocks, it's well worth the detour.

activities.text: Hike through Pororari Gorge: the Pororari River Track climbs 5 km through limestone gorges overrun with tropical vegetation and tree ferns. The deep green river contrasts with the light rock - one of the West Coast's most remarkable landscapes.

Seal and Hector's dolphin watching from Punakaiki cliffs: Punakaiki Marine Reserve (created 2014, 3,520 ha) protects this exposed coast. Hector's dolphins - the world's smallest and rarest - frequent the area, and fur seals regularly rest on rocks at the cliff base.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

visits.text: Arrowtown is 21 km from Queenstown on the Arrow River: the village preserves 60 stone buildings from the 1862 gold rush era, including Chinese miners' cabins on the riverbank. The Lakes District Museum traces the history of the prospectors who flocked here after the discovery of gold seams in 1862. In autumn (April-May), the aspens planted by pioneers blanket the streets in golden leaves.

Queenstown's lakefront (Steamer Wharf, Wakatipu shores) is the heart of the town. Lake Wakatipu has a 83-km Z-shape carved by Pleistocene glaciers - its level rises and falls 10 cm every 5 minutes due to atmospheric pressure differences, a local phenomenon called a seiche. The Queenstown Gardens on the neighbouring peninsula offer relative calm facing the lake.

Last morning in Queenstown: the Saturday market on Earnslaw Wharf (if you're leaving on a Saturday) brings together local producers from the Otago region. Otherwise, the lakefront walk from Steamer Wharf to the gardens (2 km return) is enough for a final view of the Remarkables before the airport.

activities.text: Skyline gondola up Bob's Peak (450 m above the lake): panoramic view over Queenstown, Lake Wakatipu and the Remarkables - the flat-topped mountain range that served as backdrop for Middle-earth. Luge tracks descend 800 metres from the summit - a family activity but fast-paced. The summit restaurant is open evenings for dinner with the view.

If your flight is late afternoon, one last activity is possible: bungy jumping from Kawarau Bridge (43 m, 23 km from Queenstown) - this is where the sport was first commercialised in the world in 1988. Operators run the original site. Total time: roughly 1.5 hours including wait.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

visits.text: Wai-O-Tapu - 'sacred waters' - lies 27 km south of Rotorua and covers 18 km² of active geothermal zone in the Reporoa caldera. Lady Knox Geyser erupts every morning at precisely 10:15 am - arrive by 9:45 am for a good spot. After the geyser, three walking circuits (45 min to 90 min) lead to Champagne Pool (57°C, ringed with orange sulphur deposits), the Artist's Palette, bubbling mud pools and Devil's Bath, a toxic chartreuse-green pool from sulphur and ferrous salt deposits. Allow at least 2 hours minimum.

The Redwoods Whakarewarewa Forest, on the edge of Rotorua, is a California redwood forest planted from 1901 as forestry trials. The trees have reached impressive heights - some exceed 60 m - and the understory is carpeted with silver ferns. Cycling and walking tracks traverse the entire forest. The Redwood Treewalk (suspended walkway between the canopy) is a separate paid activity.

activities.text: Māori cultural evening at Tamaki village: warriors collect visitors by bus, divide them into 'tribes' and welcome them with a full pōwhiri including the wero (challenge) ceremony. You learn poi handling, haka, traditional games, before a hāngī served in the hall. Tamaki is renowned for its passionate guides and authenticity.

Relax in the Polynesian Spa pools on the shore of Lake Rotorua: 26 pools at different temperatures fed by natural geothermal springs. The waters have distinct mineral properties depending on the source - some high in sulphur, others alkaline.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

visits.text: Pupu Springs - Te Waikoropupu - are New Zealand's largest freshwater springs and the southern hemisphere's largest cold-water springs. Visibility measurements by NIWA in 1993 recorded 63 m of transparency, approaching optically pure water - only the Weddell Sea in Antarctica does better. For local Māori these waters are wahi tapu (sacred) - swimming, drinking and smoking are strictly forbidden within the precinct. A short loop walk (1 km) follows the river to Dancing Sands spring, which appears to 'dance' from upwellings from the bottom.

Farewell Spit is a 35 km sand bar extending into the Tasman Sea from Cape Farewell, the northernmost point of the South Island. It's New Zealand's most strictly protected nature reserve - only the first 4 km are accessible to the public, the rest only via authorised guided tours. The spit hosts several thousand gannets each spring in the country's only sea-level colony, over 90 bird species, and regular sightings of pilot whale strandings.

Wharariki Beach, west of Cape Farewell, is perhaps New Zealand's most spectacular beach - not for swimming (dangerous currents) but for its wind-sculpted rock arches and giant dunes crossed on foot from the car park (20 minutes). Fur seals lounge on the rocks at the beach entrance.

activities.text: Guided 4x4 tour of Farewell Spit: authorised operators depart daily from Collingwood to cross the dunes, reach the 1869 lighthouse and observe birds at the spit's feeding stations. It's the only legal way to explore the closed portion of the reserve.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety

visits.text: The Tongariro Alpine Crossing is often cited as the world's finest one-day hike. The route covers 19.4 km and traverses the flank of Aoraki/Mount Tongariro, an active volcano - a small eruption hurled meter-wide blocks onto the track in August 2012. Budget 6 to 8 hours depending on pace. High point: Red Crater at 1,868 m.

The Emerald Lakes, three craters filled with vivid green water, are the visual highlight of the crossing. The color comes from minerals leaching from the surrounding hydrothermal environment. Caution: active fumaroles emit scalding steam around the lakes - don't leave the marked trail.

In the evening, Taupo borders New Zealand's largest lake (616 km², the size of Singapore). The lakefront offers a good base for recovering after the Crossing. If energy permits, Huka Falls 2 km north of the lake are accessible in 20 minutes - the Waikato River funnels through a 15 m wide gorge at 220,000 liters per second.

activities.text: The shuttle from Taupo or Tongariro village is the only practical way to access the track: parking at the start is limited to 4 hours and the hike is linear (point A to point B). Local operators offer return shuttles with flexible end-of-day pickup.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

visits.text: Te Anau is the essential starting point for Milford Sound. Lake Te Anau, at its foot, is the South Island's largest lake (342 km²). The town is small but well-equipped - it's the last petrol station before Milford Sound, 120 km away. Fill up here.

The Te Anau Caves, accessible from town by boat, shelter a glowworm colony in an active underground system. The caves were rediscovered in 1948 - Māori had known them for centuries but their exact location had been lost. The visit lasts 1h45 and combines a lake boat ride, cavern walk, and final cruise beneath the glowworms.

Milford Sound / Piopiotahi is technically a fjord, not a sound - carved by glaciers over 100,000 years ago. The fjord extends 16 km from its head to the Tasman Sea, with walls plunging to 512 m depth. Mitre Peak, 1,683 m, rises almost vertically from the water's edge. Rudyard Kipling dubbed it 'the eighth wonder of the world'. It rains here an average 182 days per year - in rain, hundreds of ephemeral waterfalls simultaneously appear on the cliffs.

The 2-hour cruise runs the length of the fjord to the Tasman Sea mouth. You pass Bowen Falls (160 m high), the fur seal rookery on their customary rocks, and possibly dusky dolphins - Milford hosts the world's southernmost population of wild common dolphins. The Harrison's Cove underwater observatory reveals black corals at just 10 m depth, a rare phenomenon due to the tannic freshwater layer filtering light.

The Chasm sits 10 km from Milford Sound on SH94. The Cleddau River has carved giant potholes in the granite - circular forms polished by stones rotating in the current for thousands of years. The loop from the car park takes 20 minutes.

activities.text: Take the Milford Road late afternoon to catch the Mirror Lakes - small roadside ponds that reflect the Earl Mountains in calm weather. The road then ventures into Hollyford gorges before Homer Tunnel (opened in 1954 after 19 years of intermittent work). Book your Milford Sound cruise for tomorrow morning early.

Operators offer kayaks in the fjord, departing from Milford terminal. Paddling beneath 1,200-meter walls and at the foot of waterfalls gives a sense of scale impossible to grasp from a boat. Morning sessions, before group arrivals, are quietest.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

visits.text: The East Cape coast begins at Ōpōtiki, where SH35 splits from the main highway to follow the Bay of Plenty shore for 330 km of winding road. This is where the black sand beaches appear - the dark sand comes from eroded volcanic rock from the hinterland. Slow down at every viewpoint: the coast alternates between cliffs, river mouths and isolated bays where nobody stops.

Tolaga Bay Wharf is New Zealand's longest pier at 660 metres. Built in 1929 to allow steamships to load wool and meat without running aground on the shallows, it was the economic artery of the entire coast. Walk to the end: the waters are crystal clear, you can see fish swimming between the piles, and the view of the white cliffs is striking. Some locals jump from the end at high tide.

activities.text: Hike to Cook's Cove: from the car park at Tolaga Bay entrance, this 2.5 km one-way trail leads to a secluded cove with historical panels recounting James Cook's 1769 anchorage. The captain needed fresh water and timber after five weeks at sea - he stayed six days. Spectacular cliff views from the headland.

Sunset over the bay from the wharf or Tolaga Bay beach: the black sand beaches ignite at sunset, and the sound of Pacific waves echoes in a silence that contrasts completely with the rest of the circuit. This is the Kiwi Far East - one of the remotest and quietest corners of the country.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety

visits.text: The Fullers 360 ferry leaves from Viaduct Harbour and takes 35 to 45 minutes to reach Matiatia. From the terminal, you're within walking distance or a short bus ride from most vineyards. Waiheke boasts 216 hectares of vines spread across thirty producers - the island is nicknamed 'wine island' and specializes in Bordeaux-style reds and Syrah.

Tantalus Estate stretches across 8 hectares in the Onetangi Valley, on well-draining sandy soils laid over clay and rock - a terroir renowned for age-worthy reds. The winery was built in 2013 by the Mendell-Aitken family, featuring a light-filled tasting room and an attached craft brewery. From Tantalus, Te Motu is a 15-minute walk.

Te Motu was founded in 1988 by the Dunleavy family, among Waiheke's wine pioneers. The first commercial vintage dates to 1993. The estate's vines grow on ancient Waipapa Group Jurassic soils - a geology that gives their Cabernet-Merlot blends distinctive structure.

Onetangi Beach is the island's longest, stretching about 1 km of white sand. It's a twenty-minute walk from the vineyards. Perfect for a break before catching the return ferry or enjoying the evening if you're staying overnight.

activities.text: Local operators offer guided tasting tours by minibus from Matiatia terminal. They visit 3 to 4 estates in a half-day and handle transport logistics between vineyards - useful when walking distances grow long.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

visits.text: The Waitomo Caves lie 2.5 hours south of Auckland. 'Waitomo' comes from Māori: wai (water) and tomo (hole). The limestone forming these cavities was laid down 30 million years ago when the region lay beneath the sea. Māori chief Tane Tinorau first explored the caves in the 1880s on a raft woven from flax.

The Waitomo Glowworm Cave offers a silent boat cruise beneath thousands of glowworms - actually larvae of fungus gnats (Arachnocampa luminosa), a species endemic to New Zealand and Australia. Their sticky mucus threads trap insects attracted by their bioluminescence. The illuminated ceiling resembles an inverted starry sky.

activities.text: Black Labyrinth is the introductory version of black water rafting: 3 hours in Ruakuri Cave, combining tubing on an underground river, backward leaps into rapids, and floating beneath a glowworm-lit ceiling. No experience required. The Black Abyss version (5 hours) adds a 35-meter abseil and a flying fox through the illuminated gallery.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand

visits.text: Makarora's Blue Pools are an unmissable stop on SH6 between the West Coast and Wanaka. A suspension bridge spans the Makarora River, whose glacier-fed waters turn intense turquoise. Swimming is possible but water rarely exceeds 10°C. The return walk from the car park takes 45 minutes.

Lake Wanaka stretches across 192 km² at the foot of snow-capped mountains. The village lakefront is lively in the evening - mountain glaciers visible from café terraces. That Wanaka Tree, a solitary willow growing in the lake a few meters from shore, has become an Instagram star and one of New Zealand's most published photographic subjects.

Matukituki Valley, 55 km west of Wanaka, enters Mount Aspiring / Tititea National Park. Aspiring (Tititea) peaks at 3,033 m - the only summit over 3,000 m outside the central Southern Alps. The road follows the Matukituki River between pastures, gorges, and beech forests.

The Rob Roy Glacier Track is one of the South Island's finest day hikes. The trail climbs through a narrow gorge covered in mosses and ferns before opening onto a glacial amphitheater with frontal views of Rob Roy Glacier. Avalanches regularly trigger from the walls - stay on the marked trail.

activities.text: The West Coast road to Wanaka crosses Haast Pass (562 m), UNESCO World Heritage-listed along with the rest of Te Wahipounamu. The descent from the pass crosses spectacular gorges and passes several waterfalls easily accessible from the road, notably Fantail Falls and Thunder Creek Falls.
Wanaka makes a good base for a relaxed bar evening before the active days ahead. The town is less touristy than Queenstown while offering quality restaurants and craft breweries.

For skydiving enthusiasts, Wanaka is one of the world's most beautiful tandem jump sites - the view of the lake and Southern Alps from 4,500 m is remarkable. Operators offer jumps all day in good weather.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

visits.text: Te Papa Tongarewa - 'the place of treasures of this land' - is New Zealand's national museum, opened in 1998 on the Wellington waterfront. The 36,000 m² building, set on lead-rubber seismic isolators, houses over two million objects: Māori taonga, natural fauna including the famous colossal squid, art collections and the Gallipoli exhibition created by Weta Workshop with figures 2.4 times life-size. Allow at least 3 hours minimum.

The Mount Victoria lookout (196 m) offers the best view over Wellington, its harbour and the entrance to Cook Strait. This is where Peter Jackson filmed several scenes from the Lord of the Rings trilogy. You can drive up or walk in 30 minutes from the centre. Cuba Street below, with its Bucket Fountain and alternative shops, is the capital's liveliest artery.

Zealandia Te Māra a Tāne is the world's first fully fenced, predator-free urban sanctuary. Its 225 hectares, 4 km from the CBD, shelter 40 endemic bird species including kiwi, tuatara (prehistoric reptile unchanged for 200 million years), kākā and takahē - believed extinct until its rediscovery in 1948. Since the sanctuary's opening in 1999, 18 species have been reintroduced. The night tour allows observation of kiwi in their natural element.

Old St Paul's Cathedral (1866) is a Gothic cathedral built entirely of local pine timber with kauri reinforcements - a world-unique technique dictated by the absence of available building stone during colonisation. The interior with its coloured stained glass and exposed timber frame has remarkable acoustics. It's one of New Zealand's most precious heritage buildings.

The New Zealand Parliament with its cylindrical tower nicknamed 'the Beehive' (1977, designed by architect Basil Spence) is one of the country's most recognisable buildings. Free guided tours run several times daily.

activities.text: Ride the cable car from Lambton Quay up to the Botanic Garden: Wellington's botanical gardens (26 hectares) descend in terraces to the city centre with panoramic harbour views. The cable car has operated since 1902. At the top, the Carter Observatory completes the visit.

Dinner on Cuba Street: Wellington's bohemian artery concentrates the capital's best independent restaurants, cocktail bars and cafés. Wellington's culinary scene is renowned throughout the country - proportionally to its size, the city has more cafés per capita than any other city in New Zealand.

Zealandia night tour with guide: guides equipped with red-light torches lead groups searching for kiwi, tuatara and glow-worms roaming free in the forest. The sanctuary's transformation after nightfall is spectacular - nocturnal birds take over the pathways.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
The Great Auckland-Christchurch Traverse: 22 Days Across New Zealand
New Zealand: The Essential Two-Week Journey

visits.text: The Forgotten World Highway (SH43) is New Zealand's oldest touring route - 150 km between Taumarunui and Stratford, built over more than 50 years. The road crosses four passes, traverses the Tangarakau Gorge and plunges into a single-lane tunnel - Moki Tunnel (183 m) - before arriving at Whangamomona. There are no petrol stations along these 150 km. Fill up at Taumarunui. The 12 km of gravel are easily drivable in a normal car.

Whangamomona, founded in 1895, declared itself an independent Republic in November 1989 after an administrative redistricting split the village between two regions without consulting residents. What started as a joke in the local hotel bar became a biennial celebration attracting up to 5,000 people. Successive presidents have included a goat, a poodle and a human. Buy your Republic of Whangamomona passport at the Whangamomona Hotel bar to ensure trouble-free transit.

activities.text: Detour to Mount Damper Falls: midway between Taumarunui and Whangamomona, a 3 km track leads to the North Island's tallest falls (88 m by some sources). The path passes through the former community of Aotuhia, abandoned in the 1940s after total isolation from floods.

Rail cart on Forgotten World Adventures: self-guided electric rail carts allow you to travel the historic railway line between Taumarunui and the remote Tokirima bush. A quirky way to see the landscape.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Tour - New Zealand in Its Entirety
